title: REGIONAL CODE OF HYGIENIC PRACTICE FOR THE PREPARATION AND SALE OF STREET FOODS

sections.section: REGIONAL CODE OF HYGIENIC PRACTICE FOR THE PREPARATION AND SALE OF STREET FOODS
sections.summary: Document title and jurisdiction: the Regional Code of Hygienic Practice for the Preparation and Sale of Street Foods for Latin America and the Caribbean (CXC 43R-1995).
States the Code contains requirements and practices for preparation and sale of ready‑to‑eat street foods and beverages and applies to places of preparation, points of sale and transport used.

Defines the Code's objective as ensuring foods sold in the street are safe and fit for consumption to protect public health and notes it is based on the Recommended International Code of Practice – General Principles of Food Hygiene (CXC 1-1969).

States that Codex Alimentarius definitions apply and provides specific definitions relevant to street foods (waste water, street foods, environment, authorized, foods for direct consumption, ingredient, input, organoleptic testing, food handler, perishable, sales stall).

sections.section: SECTION 4 - REQUIREMENTS FOR INPUTS AND INGREDIENTS
sections.summary: Contains requirements for inputs and ingredients covering purchasing (authorized sources, labeling, organoleptic quality, temperature control, appropriate quantities) and for transport/reception/storage (safe transport, clean vehicles, protected reception, suitable storage materials, segregation from non‑edible items, FIFO rotation, pest protection), and includes a hazard analysis note on microbiological, chemical and physical contamination risks.

Requires purchasing inputs and ingredients only from authorized or approved sources, using appropriately labeled/guaranteed packaged products, accepting items with proper organoleptic quality, keeping perishables at adequate temperatures and purchasing quantities matched to storage/preservation capacity.

sections.section: 4.2 TRANSPORT, RECEPTION AND STORAGE OF INPUTS AND INGREDIENTS
sections.summary: Specifies transport, reception and storage obligations: transport inputs in conditions that prevent chemical, physical or biological contamination and spoilage, use cleaned/disinfected vehicles and containers, receive and store items in clean protected areas with suitable shelving and containers, label and segregate edible from non‑edible products, rotate stock FIFO and protect against pests, noting hazards from improper handling and exposure.

sections.section: SECTION 5 - REQUIREMENTS FOR THE AREA OR PLACE OF PREPARATION
sections.summary: Sets out requirements for preparation areas and hygiene: indoor/outdoor areas must be designed per Codex, be clean, well lit, protected from contaminants, use potable water, have appropriate equipment and waste‑water disposal, and provide suitable hygiene facilities and cleaning/disinfection materials; additionally, it mandates food handler hygiene (health status, hair covering, short clean nails, no jewellery, no handling money and food simultaneously), handwashing, training, utensil cleaning and includes hazard/CCP notes.

Describes area requirements for food preparation (indoor and outdoor) including cleanliness, protection from contaminants, potable water supply and maintenance of equipment and containers, and notes that surrounding surfaces and water can be contamination sources requiring cleaning and potable water.

Details indoor area requirements: design and construction per Codex, adequate lighting, cleanliness, equipment of easily cleaned materials, use of potable water, regular cleaning of storage containers, proper waste‑water disposal, maintenance and sanitizing of food contact surfaces, and prohibitions on using containers or storing fuels/toxic substances in contact with food.

Outdoor food preparation areas must be kept clean and protected from sun, dust, wind and public contact; equipment (e.g., stoves) must be safely constructed and maintained, potable water must be sufficient and of public-source quality or equivalent, preparation surfaces must be 60–70 cm off the ground and suitable for the quantity prepared, and equipment/utensils must meet the same requirements as section 5.1.1 (with notes that surrounding surfaces and water are potential contamination sources).

Transported potable water must be stored in clean, non-toxic, hermetically sealed containers with a tap/stopcock; containers, utensils and work surfaces must be non-toxic, easy to clean and heat resistant where used for cooking; detergents/disinfectants should, where possible, be non-toxic and non-corrosive.

sections.section: 6.1.1.4.1Peel,  squeeze  and/or  cut,  as  appropriate,  fruits  and  vegetables  with  appropriate  and  hygienized equipment and utensils.
sections.summary: Peel, squeeze and/or cut fruits and vegetables using appropriate, hygienized equipment and utensils.

Cook foods thoroughly and protect them after cooking, observing specified time/temperature controls: hot-held foods up to 6 hours above 60°C, refrigerated foods up to one day at ≤5°C, chill hot foods to 5°C within three hours, and reheat refrigerated foods only once to 70°C immediately before consumption; prepare sandwiches and fresh salads at time of consumption when possible, keep salads with mayonnaise/cheese/ham below 5°C, keep items to be cooked later under 5°C, and avoid using leftovers by preparing only what can be sold in a day.

When serving and selling food, use disposable serviceware or ensure reusable items are washed and disinfected after each use; store leftovers and disposables in covered secure containers, wrap take-away food in unused non-contaminating materials (no printed plastics), avoid excessive handling, protect/display foods at appropriate temperatures (reheat chilled hot foods >70°C), supply condiments as single-portion units or hygienic dispensers, avoid direct hand contact with ready-to-eat foods and handling money while preparing food, and keep food-preparation areas in vehicles separate from other compartments.

Food handlers and vendors are responsible for the hygiene and safety of the food they prepare or sell, and appropriate training and observance of hygienic practices are important to prevent contamination.

sections.section: SECTION 9 - HANDLING AND DISPOSAL OF WASTE AND PEST CONTROL
sections.summary: Waste containers must be kept away from food areas, fitted with lids (preferably automatic), made of resistant, waterproof, easy-to-clean material and not placed on the floor or against walls; wastewater should be disposed of to sewage where possible and not onto the ground or surface waters; recyclable and non-recyclable wastes should be segregated per municipal rules, food waste disposed so as not to attract pests, and pest control must follow official regulations with chemical treatments by authorized personnel avoiding contamination.
